meta.clinicianNotes: Progressive hearing loss with subtle night-vision/peripheral vision clues suggestive of Usher syndrome. Learning objective: students should ask broadly enough to surface the vision history and childhood balance/motor delay — neither is something Ruby thinks to connect to her hearing. Common student issue: staying narrowly focused on the ears and never asking about vision or early developmental milestones.

patient.name: Ruby Campbell
patient.additionalNotes: Ruby is friendly and cooperative but doesn't connect her different symptoms together — she answers what's asked without volunteering the bigger picture. She wouldn't mention her night vision or clumsiness as a kid unless specifically asked, since she's never thought of them as related to her hearing.

history.reasonForAppointment: Hearing has been gradually getting worse over the past few years and she's started missing things at work, like quiet questions from clients or a phone ringing in another room.

previousHearingTest.details: Had a hearing test as a child, around age 7, after her parents noticed she was slow to respond to sounds — was told she had 'a mild loss' at the time, hearing aids were discussed but not pursued as it wasn't considered severe enough. No follow-up testing since then until now.

hearing.declineDetails: Both ears, roughly even, slowly worsening for as long as she can really remember but definitely more noticeable in the last 3-4 years. Struggles most with quiet or distant sounds and background noise.

tinnitus.details: Mild intermittent ringing, present for a few years, not a big bother.

balance.details: If asked specifically about balance as a child, she'll mention she was 'a clumsy kid', a bit late to walk steadily and often bumped into things, though her parents never had it investigated and she hasn't thought about it since. No current balance issues.

generalHealth.majorIllnesses: If asked specifically about her eyesight or vision, Ruby will mention she's had trouble seeing at night or in dim light for a few years — bumps into furniture in the dark, struggles driving at night — and was told by an optometrist a while back that her peripheral vision was 'a bit narrow' but no formal eye diagnosis was made. She wouldn't bring this up unless asked directly about vision.

noiseHistory.details: No significant occupational or recreational noise exposure.

familyHistory.details: Her younger brother also has some hearing loss, diagnosed in childhood, similar pattern to hers. No known specific genetic diagnosis in the family — no one has pursued genetic testing.

history.otherConcerns: Ruby wants to know if her hearing loss is going to keep getting worse and whether it's likely to be genetic since her brother has something similar.
